mnemonics: FINITE PATIENCE: A Family Is Nestled In The Elements, Providing A Test; He Intersects Every Neighboring Collection Enough. (Helps recall that a family of sets has the finite intersection property if every finite subfamily has a non-empty intersection, and that this is equivalent to compactness in a metric space.)
keyPoints: A metric space is compact if and only if every family of closed sets with the finite intersection property has a non-empty intersection.
The finite intersection property (F.I.P.) means that every finite subcollection of the family has a non-empty intersection.
This property provides a powerful dual characterization of compactness, often used in proofs to show the existence of a common point.
commonMistakes: Assuming the finite intersection property applies to any collection of sets, rather than specifically to closed sets, when proving a space is compact.
Forgetting that the entire family must have a non-empty intersection, not just the finite subfamilies, for the characterization to hold.
Confusing this with the definition of compactness via open covers and incorrectly applying the F.I.P. to open sets.
